did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning feature engineering model combining ResNet 50 for feature extraction and an optimized XG-Boost classifier (optimized via Grey Wolf Optimization) to classify dermal cell pictures and detect skin cancer. The model was evaluated on the ISIC 2019 dataset, achieving 98.34% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a deep learning feature engineering model (ResNet 50 and optimized XG-Boost) for skin cancer detection. It includes a 'Proposed Work' section detailing the methodology, algorithm, and evaluation on the ISIC 2019 dataset, reporting specific performance metrics (98.34% accuracy). Although published in a conference proceedings (ComPE 2021), it reports new results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Improved Skin Cancer Detection Classification Residual Network Feature Engineering
journal: 2021 International Conference on Computational Performance Evaluation (ComPE)
brief_summary: This study develops a deep learning feature engineering model for skin cancer detection and classification using dermal cell images. The proposed architecture combines ResNet 50 for block-wise feature extraction with an XG-Boost classifier optimized via Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO). The model was evaluated on the ISIC 2019 dataset, comparing its performance against various existing deep learning and machine learning approaches. The primary evaluation metrics included accuracy, precision, recall, and F-score. The proposed RES-XG-GWO model achieved a maximum accuracy of 98.34% and a precision of 97.35%. The results indicate that optimizing the XG-Boost structure and using leaky ReLU activation significantly enhances feature mapping and classification performance for skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on skin cancer detection, specifically highlighting melanoma as the deadliest type of skin cancer. The model was evaluated on the ISIC 2019 dataset for classifying dermal cell pictures and detecting skin cancer, with the primary goal of distinguishing malignant lesions (melanoma) from benign ones.

Nevus is mentioned as a benign condition that must be differentiated from melanoma due to comparable appearance. The paper references other studies (e.g., [3], [6]) that differentiate melanoma from nevus, and the ISIC 2019 dataset typically contains both classes for binary classification (benign vs. malignant).

Mentioned in the introduction as a type of skin cancer less dangerous than melanoma, but not explicitly stated as a specific class label used in the proposed model's output or ISIC 2019 binary classification task.

Mentioned in the introduction as a type of skin cancer less dangerous than melanoma, but not explicitly stated as a specific class label used in the proposed model's output or ISIC 2019 binary classification task.

all_task_types.target: Dermal cell pictures for skin cancer detection (benign vs. malignant/melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper develops a model to classify dermal cell pictures and detect skin cancer. The abstract states the purpose is 'classifying dermal cell pictures and detecting skin cancer.' The methodology uses ResNet 50 for feature extraction and an optimized XG-Boost classifier. The evaluation on the ISIC 2019 dataset reports accuracy (98.34%) and precision (97.35%), which are metrics for classification tasks. The conclusion mentions building a 'discriminatory dermoscopy picture lesion detection model' and 'classification branch network' to distinguish between classes (implied binary: melanoma vs. non-melanoma/benign based on the context of melanoma detection).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A publicly available benchmark dataset for skin lesion analysis, used in this study for evaluating the proposed deep learning feature engineering model (RES-XG-GWO) for skin cancer detection and classification.

use.training: Used for training the proposed RES-XG-GWO model (ResNet 50 feature extraction and optimized XG-Boost classifier).
use.validation: Used for validation/evaluation of the model's performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F-score).
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation of the model's performance metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F-score) and compared against other models in tables without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019 Dataset.notes: The proposed RES-XG-GWO model achieved the highest performance metrics. The paper notes a significant improvement of 10-12% over existing approaches. Table 2 compares activation functions for the proposed model, showing Leaky ReLU yielded the best results (Accuracy: 98.344, Precision: 97.34, Recall: 98.5, F-score: 98.56). Table 3 shows epoch tuning, where 180 epochs yielded the peak performance for the proposed model.

architecture_used.main_model: RES-XG-GWO (ResNet 50 for feature extraction combined with XG-Boost classifier optimized via Grey Wolf Optimization)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained ResNet 50 used as a function extractor for block-wise feature engineering

architecture_details.activation_function: Leaky ReLU (identified as optimal in Table II)
architecture_details.other_components: Gradient & Feature Adaptive Model for segmentation (mentioned in workflow diagram), K-means clustering (mentioned in related work)

training_details.optimizer: Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO) used to optimize XG-Boost structure

preprocessing_techniques: Preprocessing step mentioned in the proposed algorithm (Step 4: P <- Preprocessing (Ni)) but specific techniques are not detailed in the text.
Reference to 'removal of artifacts' as a general step in CAD methods found in literature (Section II).
Reference to 'Gaussian filter' for noise removal in related work (Section II, reference [3]).
Reference to 'thresholding & masking segmentation' and 'Gradient & Feature Adaptive Model' for lesion segmentation in the workflow diagram description (Section II).
Comparison of segmentation methods including SRM, Iterative stochastic, Multilevel thresholding, and adaptive thresholding in related work (Section II).
conclusions: The proposed deep learning feature engineering model (RES-XG-GWO), which combines ResNet 50 for block-wise feature extraction and an XG-Boost classifier optimized via Grey Wolf Optimization, achieved a maximum accuracy of 98.34% and a precision of 97.35% on the ISIC 2019 dataset.
The use of Leaky ReLU activation function significantly enhanced feature mapping and classification performance compared to other activation functions like Tanh, Sigmoid, and ReLU.

The proposed method substantially improves performance over existing deep learning and machine learning approaches (such as Random Forest, XG-Boost, LSTM-RNN, and standard Residual Networks) by optimizing the XG-Boost structure and refining residual block-wise features.
A practitioner may use this model-driven architecture to rapidly develop deep learning models for skin cancer prediction.

The study demonstrates that optimizing the classifier structure and utilizing specific activation functions can effectively distinguish between malignant melanoma and benign lesions in dermoscopy images.
important_remarks: The paper contains significant internal inconsistencies: the abstract and conclusion state the model achieved 98.34% accuracy, but Table I lists the proposed model's accuracy as 98.344% while listing 'Residual network' at 98.5%, yet the text claims the proposed method is superior. Additionally, Table III shows epoch tuning results peaking at 98.344% at 180 epochs, but the surrounding text and other tables show lower values (e.g., ~83%) for other epochs, suggesting potential data fabrication or reporting errors.

There is a contradiction in the optimization algorithm description: the abstract and title mention Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO), but the proposed algorithm pseudocode (Step 13) and Table III caption refer to Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO).
The study relies solely on the ISIC 2019 dataset without external validation, limiting the generalizability of the results to other populations or imaging devices.

The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, making it impossible to assess the statistical significance of the reported improvements over baseline models.
The dataset split (training/validation/testing) is not explicitly defined, raising concerns about potential data leakage or overfitting, especially given the high reported accuracy.
The preprocessing steps are vaguely described ('Preprocessing (Ni)') without detailing specific techniques used for artifact removal or normalization, hindering reproducibility.

The comparison with existing methods in Table I includes models (e.g., LSTM-RNN, Residual network) with metrics that appear inconsistent with their typical performance on this dataset, and the baseline 'Residual network' accuracy (98.5%) is higher than the proposed model (98.344%), contradicting the claim of substantial improvement.
